perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the following terms have the following meanings: 1.3.1 a. While Selling Shareholder is a resident of California, "Restricted Area" shall mean all counties and cities in the States of California and Nevada in which SAI, as of the Effective Time, carries on the Business. The covenant not to compete in the Restricted Area shall be construed as a series of separate covenants with regard to such city or county in the States of California and in Nevada. b. If Selling Shareholder ceases to be a resident of California, immediately upon Selling Shareholder's change of residency "Restricted Area" shall mean each state in which either Dollar Tree or SAI, as of the Effective Time, has a store or otherwise carries on the Business. The covenant not to compete in the Restricted Area shall be viewed as a series of separate covenants with regard to each state where either Dollar Tree or SAI carries on the Business.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the following terms have the following meanings: 1.3.1 a. While Selling Shareholder is a resident of California, "Restricted Area" shall mean all counties and cities in the States of California and Nevada in which SAI, as of the Effective Time, conducts activities. The covenant not to compete in the Restricted Area shall be construed as a series of separate covenants with regard to such city or county in the States of California and in Nevada. b. If Selling Shareholder ceases to be a resident of California, immediately upon Selling Shareholder's change of residency "Restricted Area" shall mean each state in which either Dollar Tree or SAI, as of the Effective Time, has a store or otherwise carries on the Business. The covenant not to compete in the Restricted Area shall be viewed as a series of separate covenants with regard to each state where either Dollar Tree or SAI carries on the Business.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "carries on the Business" to "conducts activities," the scope of the restricted area becomes ambiguous. "Conducts activities" is much broader, which introduces a contradiction. It creates uncertainty around whether ancillary actions like marketing, online sales, or even basic business communications would constitute "activities", thereby extending the restricted area beyond physical store locations and potentially affecting online business or out-of-state marketing efforts. The contradiction arises because section 1.1.1 defines the restricted activities by a particular area.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Selling Shareholder agrees, during the period ending five years from the Effective Time ("Restricted Period"), neither Selling Shareholder nor any affiliate of Selling Shareholder shall conduct any of the following activities described in Sections 1.1.1, 1.1.2, and 1.1.3: 1.1.1 engage in Restricted Activities within the Restricted Area (as defined below); or 1.1.2 operate or promote within the Restricted Area a store using (or at least a 500 square foot area within a store dedicated to) a single price point retail concept selling goods at a single price point at or below $2.00; or 1.1.3 operate any variety, clearance, or close-out retail store in the same shopping center as any retail location of SAI existing as of the Effective Time or, in the case of an existing store of SAI not in a shopping center, within one-quarter mile of such retail location.
perturbation.justification: The change from "carries on the Business" to "conducts activities" broadens the scope of the "Restricted Area" definition in section 1.3.1. This creates a direct conflict with the contradicted text, specifically sections 1.1.1, 1.1.2, and 1.1.3, which define restricted activities based on the Restricted Area. The broader definition introduced by "conducts activities" makes the restricted activities ambiguous and potentially encompasses a wider range of actions than originally intended. This creates uncertainty and a strong contradiction, because it is unclear what activities are now prohibited within the Restricted Area.

perturbation.original_text: Selling Shareholder agrees to cooperate in any reasonable manner with any reasonable requests by Dollar Tree from time to time to verify compliance with the provisions of this Agreement, provided, however, Dollar Tree shall reimburse Selling Shareholder for Selling Shareholder's reasonable out-of-pocket costs in complying with such request.
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Shareholder agrees to cooperate in any reasonable manner with any reasonable requests by Dollar Tree from time to time to verify compliance with the provisions of this Agreement, provided, however, Dollar Tree may reimburse Selling Shareholder for Selling Shareholder's documented expenses in complying with such request.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall reimburse" to "may reimburse" fundamentally alters Dollar Tree's obligation. Originally, reimbursement for reasonable out-of-pocket costs was mandatory. Now, it's discretionary and subject to documentation requirements. This directly contradicts the 'reasonableness' standard initially established for Dollar Tree's requests. A 'reasonable request' now can be coupled with a potential denial of reimbursement, creating a scenario where Selling Shareholder might refuse cooperation due to the risk of uncompensated expenses. Also, the term "documented expenses" is not clarified, creating further vagueness. This creates conflicting obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Selling Shareholder agrees to cooperate in any reasonable manner with any reasonable requests by Dollar Tree from time to time to verify compliance with the provisions of this Agreement, provided, however, Dollar Tree may reimburse Selling Shareholder for Selling Shareholder's documented expenses in complying with such request.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates reimbursement ('shall reimburse') for reasonable out-of-pocket costs, while the changed text makes reimbursement discretionary ('may reimburse') and introduces a documentation requirement. This creates a direct conflict with the concept of 'reasonable requests,' as the Selling Shareholder now bears the risk of non-reimbursement for complying with such requests. The contradicted text is identical to the changed text, further highlighting the contradiction. This change in obligation directly impacts the Selling Shareholder's willingness to cooperate, creating a strong inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: As used herein, the term "affiliate" with respect to any person shall mean any person or entity which controls such person, which that person controls, or which is under common control with that person. In the case of Selling Shareholder, the term "affiliate" shall include the spouse, sibling, or lineal descendant or ancestor of Selling Shareholder, a trust for the benefit of any of the preceding, or any corporation, partnership, joint venture, or other entity which the Selling Shareholder, the spouse, sibling, or lineal descendant or ancestor of Selling Shareholder, or a trust for the benefit of any of the preceding, controls.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, the term "associate" with respect to any person shall mean any person or entity which controls such person, which that person controls, or which is under common control with that person. In the case of Selling Shareholder, the term "associate" shall include the spouse, sibling, or lineal descendant or ancestor of Selling Shareholder, a trust for the benefit of any of the preceding, or any corporation, partnership, joint venture, or other entity which the Selling Shareholder, the spouse, sibling, or lineal descendant or ancestor of Selling Shareholder, or a trust for the benefit of any of the preceding, controls.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'affiliate' to 'associate' and NOT updating the term "affiliate" in the rest of the document creates a contradiction because the document repeatedly uses the term 'affiliate' in sections 1.1 and 6.1, implying a specific and broad definition. This change creates confusion as to which definition, if any, applies. This change contradicts the sections defining prohibited activities in section 1.1
perturbation.contradicted_text: Selling Shareholder agrees, during the period ending five years from the Effective Time ("Restricted Period"), neither Selling Shareholder nor any affiliate of Selling Shareholder shall conduct any of the following activities described in Sections 1.1.1, 1.1.2, and 1.1.3: 1.1.1 engage in Restricted Activities within the Restricted Area (as defined below); or 1.1.2 operate or promote within the Restricted Area a store using (or at least a 500 square foot area within a store dedicated to) a single price point retail concept selling goods at a single price point at or below $2.00; or 1.1.3 operate any variety, clearance, or close-out retail store in the same shopping center as any retail location of SAI existing as of the Effective Time or, in the case of an existing store of SAI not in a shopping center, within one-quarter mile of such retail location.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'affiliate,' and the contradicted text uses this defined term. The changed text replaces 'affiliate' with 'associate' in the definition. This means the contract now uses 'affiliate' without a clear definition, while 'associate' now has a definition. Since the contradicted text refers to 'affiliate' specifically, the change creates a contradiction because it is unclear whether the original definition of 'affiliate' still applies or if 'affiliate' is now meant to be read as 'associate,' altering the scope of restricted activities by Selling Shareholder and their connections.

perturbation.original_text: The Selling Shareholder acknowledges that Dollar Tree shall have no adequate remedy at law and that irreparable injury may result to Dollar Tree in the event Selling Shareholder violates or breaches any of the above agreements, covenants, or restrictions. Therefore, it is hereby agreed that upon any breach of any of the above covenants, agreements, or restrictions, Dollar Tree shall be entitled, in addition to any other remedies available, to an injunction to restrain the violation or breach of any of the terms of such covenants, agreements, or restrictions by Selling Shareholder, and that no bond shall be required in procuring any such injunction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Selling Shareholder acknowledges that Dollar Tree may not have an adequate remedy at law and that irreparable inconvenience may result to Dollar Tree in the event Selling Shareholder violates or breaches any of the above agreements, covenants, or restrictions. Therefore, it is hereby agreed that upon any alleged breach of any of the above covenants, agreements, or restrictions, Dollar Tree shall be entitled, in addition to any other remedies available, to seek an injunction to restrain the violation or breach of any of the terms of such covenants, agreements, or restrictions by Selling Shareholder, and that a bond may be required in procuring any such injunction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall have no adequate remedy' to 'may not have an adequate remedy', changing 'irreparable injury' to 'irreparable inconvenience,' changing 'upon any breach' to 'upon any alleged breach', changing 'shall be entitled... to an injunction' to 'shall be entitled... to seek an injunction', and changing 'no bond shall be required' to 'a bond may be required' significantly weakens Dollar Tree's legal standing. Originally, the clause presumed irreparable harm and guaranteed injunctive relief without a bond. The modified language introduces uncertainty. This contradicts the stated intention of ensuring Dollar Tree receives the value of the merger. If Dollar Tree needs to 'seek' an injunction and 'may' be required to post a bond, the enforcement becomes less certain and more costly. This change contradicts section WHEREAS, which states "Selling Shareholder further acknowledges Dollar Tree may suffer irreparable damage should Selling Shareholder breach this Agreement."
perturbation.contradicted_text: WHEREAS, Selling Shareholder further acknowledges Dollar Tree may suffer irreparable damage should Selling Shareholder breach this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text firmly established Dollar Tree's right to injunctive relief without a bond in case of a breach. The changed text introduces uncertainty by stating Dollar Tree 'may not' have an adequate remedy, 'may' suffer irreparable inconvenience, might only 'seek' an injunction, and 'may' be required to post a bond. This directly contradicts the 'WHEREAS' clause stating that Dollar Tree 'may suffer irreparable damage should Selling Shareholder breach this Agreement,' as it undermines the assurance of immediate and unencumbered injunctive relief that would typically follow from such irreparable damage. The change weakens Dollar Tree's position and makes enforcement less certain, creating an in-text contradiction.
